_meta.purpose: Semantic navigation map for EU AI Act — used to ground an LLM into the correct articles given a user AI system description.
_meta.legal_baseline_note: Based on Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 as published in the Official Journal of the European Union on 12 July 2024. Any amendments after this date are not reflected in this map.
_meta.how_to_use: Follow the reasoning_workflow steps. Match the user's AI system against the decision_tree_questions, then use the article references to retrieve exact text from the chapter JSON files.
_meta.retrieval_instruction: When an article is identified, read it from its source file using the article_lookup.json mapping. Always cite the article number and paragraph id (e.g. Article 9, §9.001).

reasoning_workflow.description: Step-by-step reasoning process an LLM must follow to identify applicable articles.

steps.label: Determine applicability (scope)
steps.question: Does this AI system fall within the scope of the EU AI Act?
steps.guidance: Check Article 2 (Scope). Exclusions include: national security / military use (Art. 2(3)), scientific R&D only (Art. 2(6)), personal non-professional use (Art. 2(10)), open-source unless high-risk or Article 5/50 applies (Art. 2(12)).
steps.if_out_of_scope: No obligations apply. State the relevant exclusion from Article 2.

steps.label: Classify risk level — MANDATORY top-down hierarchy
steps.question: What is the risk classification of this AI system?
steps.critical_rule: ALWAYS evaluate sub-steps in the exact order listed. Each sub-step is a hard gate: if a system triggers 2a (prohibited), the analysis stops — do NOT continue to 2b or 2c. Only advance to the next sub-step if the current one does NOT apply.

sub_steps.label: PROHIBITION CHECK — hard stop
sub_steps.question: Does the system use any of the 8 prohibited practices listed in Article 5?
trigger_examples: Subliminal or manipulative techniques that impair free will (Art 5(1)(a))
Exploitation of vulnerabilities of age, disability, or economic situation (Art 5(1)(b))
Social scoring by public authorities or private operators for unrelated purposes (Art 5(1)(c))
Criminal risk assessment purely from profiling or personality traits (Art 5(1)(d))
Untargeted scraping of facial images to build biometric databases (Art 5(1)(e))
Emotion recognition in workplace or educational settings (Art 5(1)(f))
Biometric categorisation to infer sensitive attributes: race, political opinions, religion, sexual orientation (Art 5(1)(g))
Real-time remote biometric identification in public spaces by law enforcement (Art 5(1)(h))
sub_steps.if_triggered: STOP. The system cannot be placed on the market or put into service. State the specific Article 5 paragraph. Do not proceed to sub-step 2b.

sub_steps.label: HIGH-RISK — Annex I pathway (safety component of regulated product)
sub_steps.question: Is the AI system a safety component of a product covered by EU harmonisation legislation listed in Annex I (e.g. Machinery Regulation, Medical Devices Regulation, Radio Equipment Directive, Aviation, Vehicles)?
sub_steps.if_triggered: Classify as HIGH-RISK. Third-party notified body conformity assessment is required. Proceed to Step 3 (operator role).

sub_steps.label: HIGH-RISK — Annex III pathway (standalone high-risk domain)
sub_steps.question: Does the system fall within one of the 8 high-risk domains listed in Annex III?
sub_steps.if_triggered: Provisionally classify as HIGH-RISK. MANDATORY: proceed immediately to sub-step 2d to determine whether the Art 6(3) derogation applies before confirming classification.
sub_steps.if_not_triggered: Continue to sub-step 2d is not required. Proceed to sub-step 2e.

sub_steps.label: Art 6(3) DEROGATION — strict self-assessment gate
sub_steps.question: Can the provider credibly document that this Annex III system does NOT pose a significant risk of harm to health, safety, or fundamental rights — for example because it performs a purely ancillary or preparatory task?
sub_steps.critical_implementation_note: This derogation is a legal risk, NOT a convenience escape. The tool MUST NOT simply tell the user 'it is fine.' If the AI system appears in Annex III, the DEFAULT classification is HIGH-RISK. The derogation requires: (1) the provider formally documents the reasoning BEFORE market placement; (2) the documentation must withstand scrutiny by national market surveillance authorities; (3) misclassification (claiming derogation when system is actually high-risk) is a violation of Art 6 and exposes the provider to fines up to €15,000,000 or 3% of global annual turnover under Art 99(3).
sub_steps.derogation_example: A tool that only spell-checks a CV before it reaches a recruitment AI is ancillary and may qualify. A tool that scores or ranks candidates does NOT qualify — it is squarely within Annex III(4).
sub_steps.required_action_if_claimed: The Art 6(3) derogation is only available if ALL FOUR of the following conditions are satisfied simultaneously — instruct the user to verify each one and document compliance in writing BEFORE market placement: (1) the AI system performs a narrow procedural or preparatory task only; (2) the AI system is intended only to improve the result of a previously completed human activity (it does not initiate or determine the activity itself); (3) the AI system detects decision patterns or deviations from prior human decisions but does not itself make any decision, recommendation, or ranking; (4) the AI system is designed for a preparatory task only and cannot influence a substantive decision affecting persons. PROFILING VOID RULE: if the system performs profiling within the meaning of Article 3(68) of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (GDPR), the Art 6(3) derogation is automatically void — the system remains high-risk regardless of any ancillary or preparatory claims. Instruct the user to also: (a) document the specific functional reasons each condition is met; (b) retain this documentation for authorities on request; (c) register the self-assessment outcome in the EU database where required under Art 49.
sub_steps.if_derogation_valid: Reclassify as non-high-risk. Still check sub-step 2e (transparency obligations may still apply).
sub_steps.if_derogation_not_valid_or_uncertain: Maintain HIGH-RISK classification. Proceed to Step 3.

sub_steps.label: TRANSPARENCY ONLY (Article 50) — independent check
sub_steps.question: Does the system: (a) interact conversationally with humans (chatbot/voice assistant); (b) generate synthetic images, video or audio (deepfake); (c) perform emotion recognition or biometric categorisation; or (d) generate text on matters of public interest?
sub_steps.note: This check applies to ALL systems that reach this point, including systems that passed the Art 6(3) derogation. A non-high-risk system can still carry Art 50 transparency obligations.
sub_steps.if_triggered: Apply Art 50 disclosure obligations only. No full high-risk regime unless sub-step 2b or 2c also triggered.

sub_steps.label: GENERAL-PURPOSE AI MODEL (GPAI) — independent parallel track
sub_steps.question: Is this a foundation model / LLM / multimodal model placed on the market independently with broad capabilities across many tasks?
sub_steps.note: GPAI obligations run on a SEPARATE TRACK from the high-risk classification. A GPAI provider may simultaneously face GPAI obligations (Arts 51–55) AND high-risk obligations if their model is integrated into a high-risk AI system. The systemic risk threshold is training compute > 10^25 FLOPs.
sub_steps.if_triggered: Apply GPAI obligations (Arts 53–55). If systemic risk threshold met, apply Art 55 additional obligations.

sub_steps.label: MINIMAL RISK — default
sub_steps.question: Has the system passed all checks above without triggering any category?
sub_steps.if_triggered: No mandatory obligations under the AI Act. Voluntary codes of conduct encouraged under Art 95.

steps.label: Identify operator role
steps.question: What is the user's role in relation to this AI system?
steps.guidance: Roles are defined in Article 3. A provider develops/places on market. A deployer uses it under own authority. Others: importer, distributor, authorised representative.

steps.label: Identify specific obligations
steps.question: What are the concrete obligations for this role and risk level?
steps.guidance: Use the obligations_by_role and obligations_by_type sections. For high-risk: Articles 8–27 (providers), Article 26 (deployers). For GPAI: Articles 53–55. For transparency: Article 50.

steps.label: Check conformity and registration
steps.question: Are there conformity assessment, CE marking, or registration requirements?
steps.guidance: High-risk systems require conformity assessment (Art. 43), EU declaration of conformity (Art. 47), CE marking (Art. 48), and registration in the EU database (Art. 49, 71).

steps.label: Check penalties exposure
steps.question: What penalties apply if obligations are not met?
steps.guidance: See Article 99 (fines up to €35M / 7% global turnover for prohibited AI, €15M / 3% for high-risk violations, €7.5M / 1.5% for incorrect information). Article 100 for public bodies. Article 101 for GPAI providers.

prohibited.label: Prohibited AI Practices
prohibited.summary: AI systems listed in Article 5 are completely banned. Placing on the market, putting into service, or using them is forbidden.

practices.description: Subliminal, manipulative or deceptive techniques that distort behaviour causing harm
Exploiting vulnerabilities of specific groups (age, disability, social/economic situation)
Social scoring by private operators for unrelated contexts
Assessing risk of committing a crime purely based on profiling or personality traits
Untargeted scraping of facial images from internet/CCTV to build databases
Emotion recognition in workplace or educational institutions
Biometric categorisation to infer sensitive attributes (race, political opinions, religion, sexual orientation, etc.)
Real-time remote biometric identification in public spaces by law enforcement (with narrow exceptions)

high_risk.label: High-Risk AI Systems
high_risk.summary: Two pathways to high-risk classification: (1) AI components in safety-regulated products under Annex I legislation; (2) standalone AI systems in the 8 domains listed in Annex III. High-risk systems face the heaviest obligations (Articles 8–49).
high_risk.classification_articles_note: Article 7 empowers the Commission to amend Annex III via delegated acts. Developers must classify against Annex III as it currently stands — Article 7 is not a classification tool for operators.

annex_I_pathway.description: AI systems that are safety components of products covered by EU harmonisation legislation listed in Annex I, Section A (e.g. Machinery Regulation, Medical Devices, Radio Equipment, etc.). These require third-party conformity assessment.

annex_III_pathway.description: Standalone AI systems in one of the 8 high-risk domains listed in Annex III. Providers may self-assess unless it involves biometrics or law enforcement (which require notified body).

domains.name: Biometrics
domains.description: Remote biometric identification systems; biometric categorisation by sensitive attributes; emotion recognition
domains.note: Permitted only where Union or national law allows

domains.name: Critical Infrastructure
domains.description: Safety components in management/operation of critical digital infrastructure, road traffic, water, gas, heating, electricity supply

domains.name: Education and Vocational Training
domains.description: Systems to determine access/admission; evaluate learning outcomes; assess education level; monitor/detect prohibited behaviour in tests

domains.name: Employment and Workers Management
domains.description: Recruitment/selection systems; systems affecting work conditions, promotion, termination; performance monitoring; task allocation by behaviour/traits

domains.name: Essential Private and Public Services
domains.description: Public benefit/assistance eligibility; creditworthiness / credit scoring (except fraud detection); insurance pricing (life & health); emergency call prioritisation / dispatch

domains.name: Law Enforcement
domains.description: Assessing victim risk; polygraph / lie detection; evidence reliability assessment; recidivism / re-offending risk; criminal profiling
domains.note: Permitted only where Union or national law allows

domains.name: Migration, Asylum and Border Control
domains.description: Polygraph tools; risk assessment for border crossing persons; asylum/visa/residence permit examination; detection/identification of persons
domains.note: Permitted only where Union or national law allows

domains.name: Administration of Justice and Democratic Processes
domains.description: AI assisting judicial authorities with fact/law research; AI influencing election outcomes or voting behaviour

not_high_risk_exception.description: A provider may determine an Annex III system is NOT high-risk if it does not pose a significant risk of harm (Article 6(3)). The provider must document the assessment BEFORE placing the system on the market. Notification to the AI Office or national authority is mandatory only if requested or during EU database registration (Article 49) — there is no standalone Annex for this procedure.
strict_implementation_rules: DEFAULT is high-risk: any system matching an Annex III domain is HIGH-RISK unless the provider can actively demonstrate otherwise.
The tool MUST NOT confirm derogation on its own. It must prompt the user to provide the specific functional justification for why the system is ancillary or preparatory.

FOUR-CONDITION TEST (all must be satisfied): (1) narrow procedural or preparatory task only; (2) intended only to improve the result of a previously completed human activity; (3) detects decision patterns or deviations but does not itself make any decision, recommendation, or ranking; (4) designed for a preparatory task only — cannot influence a substantive decision affecting persons.

PROFILING VOID RULE: if the system performs profiling within the meaning of Article 3(68) of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (GDPR), the Art 6(3) derogation is automatically void. The system remains high-risk regardless of any ancillary or preparatory claims.
The documentation must be written, retained, and available to authorities on request. A verbal or informal assessment is insufficient.
Misclassification penalty: up to €15,000,000 or 3% of global annual turnover (Article 99(3)).
Example of valid derogation: a spell-checker pre-processing CVs before they reach a separate recruitment scoring tool.
Example of INVALID derogation: any tool that ranks, scores, filters, or recommends candidates — this is squarely within Annex III(4) regardless of whether a human makes the final decision.

limited_risk_transparency.label: Transparency Obligations (Limited Risk)
limited_risk_transparency.summary: AI systems that interact with humans, generate synthetic content, or appear human must meet disclosure requirements under Article 50. No full high-risk regime applies.

obligations.description: Chatbots / conversational AI must disclose they are AI to users (unless obvious from context)
Operators deploying deep-fake or synthetic media must disclose the content is AI-generated
Emotion recognition and biometric categorisation systems must notify persons being processed
Providers of GPAI generating text published for public information (news etc.) must mark it machine-generated

general_purpose_ai_model.label: General-Purpose AI Models (GPAI)
general_purpose_ai_model.summary: Foundation models / LLMs / multi-modal models with broad capability are regulated under Chapter V (Articles 51–56). Systemic-risk models (above 10^25 FLOPs) face additional obligations.
general_purpose_ai_model.systemic_risk_threshold: 10^25 floating point operations (FLOPs) used in training

minimal_risk.label: Minimal / No Risk AI Systems
minimal_risk.summary: All AI systems not falling into prohibited, high-risk, or transparency categories. No mandatory requirements under the AI Act, but voluntary codes of conduct are encouraged (Article 95).

provider.definition: A natural or legal person (or public authority) that develops an AI system or general-purpose AI model and places it on the market or puts it into service under their own name or trademark — including where this is done free of charge. (Article 3(3))

key_obligations_high_risk.obligation: Transparency — instructions for use to deployers

key_obligations_gpai.obligation: Publicly available training-data summary (AI Office template — not a numbered annex)

deployer.definition: Any natural or legal person (or public authority) using an AI system under their own authority, except for personal non-professional use. (Article 3(4))

key_obligations_high_risk.obligation: Assign human oversight to competent persons
Inform provider of risks and incidents
Notify affected workers about AI use
Fundamental rights impact assessment (public bodies, certain deployers)

importer.definition: A natural or legal person established in the Union that places on the market an AI system bearing the name/trademark of a person established outside the Union. (Article 3(6))

key_obligations.obligation: Do not place non-compliant systems on market

distributor.definition: Any natural or legal person in the supply chain (not provider or importer) that makes an AI system available on the Union market. (Article 3(7))

key_obligations.obligation: Verify CE marking and documentation before supply
Do not store/transport in ways that compromise conformity
Inform provider or importer of non-compliance

authorised_representative.definition: A natural or legal person established in the Union with a written mandate from a non-EU provider to act on their behalf. (Article 3(5))

key_obligations.obligation: Register in EU database on behalf of provider
Hold EU declaration of conformity and technical documentation available
Inform provider to take corrective action

product_manufacturer.definition: Placing an AI system on the market or putting it into service together with their product under their own name/trademark. Treated as provider. (Article 25(1))

critical_terms.summary: Machine-based system designed to operate with varying levels of autonomy, that may exhibit adaptiveness, and that infers from inputs how to generate outputs such as predictions, content, recommendations, or decisions.
AI model trained on large amounts of data with broad capability that can serve a variety of tasks; placed on market independently.
Develops and/or places AI system on market or puts it into service under own name/trademark.
Uses an AI system under own authority (not personal non-professional use).
Classified by Article 6 using Annex I or Annex III pathway.
First making available on Union market.
Supply for first use in Union.
Use not intended but foreseeable from behaviour of humans.
Use for which the AI system is intended by the provider.
Conformity assessment body designated under applicable legislation.
Risk specific to high-impact GPAI models with significant impact on Union market.

high_risk_obligations_detail.description: All obligations in Articles 8–15 apply to the high-risk AI system. Articles 16–27 specify who bears each obligation (provider vs deployer).

technical_requirements.title: Risk management system
technical_requirements.summary: Continuous iterative process: identify and analyse risks, estimate and evaluate residual risks, adopt risk management measures. Must consider both intended use AND reasonably foreseeable misuse.

technical_requirements.title: Data and data governance
technical_requirements.summary: Training, validation, testing datasets must be subject to data governance. Must be relevant, representative, free of errors where possible, with appropriate statistical properties. Special rules for sensitive data (Art 10(5)).

technical_requirements.title: Technical documentation
technical_requirements.summary: Comprehensive documentation before placing on market. Content specified in Annex IV.

technical_requirements.title: Record-keeping
technical_requirements.summary: High-risk AI systems must enable automatic logging of events. Log retention by deployers.

technical_requirements.title: Transparency and information to deployers
technical_requirements.summary: Must come with instructions for use enabling deployers to understand capabilities, limitations, performance, and proper operation. Requirements are fully contained within Article 13 — there is no standalone Annex for instructions for use in the final Act.

technical_requirements.title: Human oversight
technical_requirements.summary: Systems must be designed to allow effective human oversight. Risk minimisation must be possible by persons to whom oversight is assigned. Includes monitor, intervene, stop capabilities.

technical_requirements.title: Accuracy, robustness and cybersecurity
technical_requirements.summary: Must achieve appropriate levels of accuracy (declared in instructions), be robust against errors and third-party manipulation (adversarial attacks, model poisoning, data poisoning).

provider_organisational_obligations.title: Obligations of providers
provider_organisational_obligations.summary: Master list of all provider obligations by reference

provider_organisational_obligations.title: Quality management system
provider_organisational_obligations.summary: Documented QMS covering design, development, testing, monitoring. Proportionate to organisation size.

provider_organisational_obligations.title: Documentation keeping
provider_organisational_obligations.summary: Keep technical documentation and QMS docs for 10 years after last placing on market.

provider_organisational_obligations.title: Automatically generated logs
provider_organisational_obligations.summary: Keep logs generated automatically for minimum 6 months (or per applicable legislation).

provider_organisational_obligations.title: Corrective actions
provider_organisational_obligations.summary: If non-conformity found: withdraw, disable, recall. Notify market surveillance authorities and deployers/users in affected Member States.

provider_organisational_obligations.title: Cooperation with authorities
provider_organisational_obligations.summary: Provide all information required to demonstrate conformity; cooperate with corrective action requests.

deployer_specific_obligations.title: Obligations of deployers of high-risk AI systems
deployer_specific_obligations.summary: Use per instructions, ensure human oversight, monitor, keep logs, inform affected workers.

deployer_specific_obligations.title: Fundamental rights impact assessment
deployer_specific_obligations.summary: Required for: public authorities, private operators using HR AI for creditworthiness/life insurance/emergency services/biometric identification. Must be done before deployment and registered.

conformity_and_market_access.description: How high-risk AI systems are authorised for the EU market.

articles.40: Harmonised standards — presumption of conformity if EN standards followed
articles.41: Common specifications — Commission may issue binding technical specs
articles.42: Presumption of conformity for specific requirements
articles.43: Conformity assessment — self-assessment (internal control per Annex VI) OR notified-body assessment per Annex VII. Notified body mandatory for biometric systems and safety-critical Annex I products.
articles.44: Certificates — issued by notified bodies, valid up to 5 years
articles.46: Derogation — national authority can authorise specific system before conformity assessment for law enforcement / national security emergencies
articles.47: EU declaration of conformity — provider declares compliance, content in Annex V
articles.48: CE marking — affixed once EU declaration of conformity complete; CE marking rules are in Annex IX (not Annex VII — Annex VII is the notified-body QMS assessment procedure).
articles.49: Registration — in EU database (Art 71). Providers before placing on market; deployers for certain systems (e.g. Art 6(1) biometric identification).

annexes.annex_VII.json: Conformity assessment based on quality management system — notified body

obligations.50_1: Providers of AI systems interacting with humans must ensure those systems identified as AI (unless obvious to a reasonably well-informed person). Exception: law enforcement with national authorization.
obligations.50_2: Providers of GPAI generating text on matters of public interest must mark it machine-generated (applies to online platforms under DSA).
obligations.50_3: Providers of emotion recognition or biometric categorisation systems must notify users being processed.
obligations.50_4: Deployers of deep-fake/synthetic video, audio, image, text generation must label as AI-generated. Exception: clearly artistic, creative, satirical purposes with appropriate disclosure.

classification.other_criteria: Commission may designate model with systemic risk based on other criteria (capabilities, market reach)

obligations: Technical documentation per Annex XI before market placement
Provide information to downstream AI system providers per Annex XII
Perform model evaluation (adversarial testing) per Article 55(1)(a)
Track, document and report serious incidents to AI Office

codes_of_practice.summary: Providers may comply with Art 53/55 obligations via Commission-approved Codes of Practice

innovation_and_sandboxes.summary: AI regulatory sandboxes allow providers (especially SMEs and start-ups) to develop and test innovative AI in controlled environment with regulatory support and temporary relaxations. Real-world testing also possible under Article 60–61.
innovation_and_sandboxes.sme_support: Article 62 provides special measures: reduced fees, priority access, dedicated guidance, administrative simplification.

ai_office.summary: Established within Commission; oversees GPAI models, coordinates enforcement, develops guidance.

eu_ai_board.summary: National competent authorities + Commission; coordinates consistent application of AI Act across Member States.

eu_database.summary: Public database of registered high-risk AI systems. Required registration before/upon market placement.

market_surveillance.summary: Post-market monitoring, incident reporting, authority powers to inspect and withdraw non-compliant systems.

right_to_explanation.summary: Persons subject to high-risk AI decisions have right to explanation from deployer.

right_to_complain.summary: Any natural person can lodge complaint with national market surveillance authority.

whistleblower.summary: Persons reporting infringements protected under EU whistleblowing law.

fines.max_fine: €35,000,000 or 7% of global annual turnover (whichever is higher)

fines.note: For SMEs including start-ups, fines shall be the LOWER of the percentage or absolute amount specified — not the higher. This is the inverse of the rule for large companies.

fines.max_fine: €15,000,000 or 3% of global annual turnover for general obligations; €3,000,000 or 1% for incorrect information

annex_I.json.title: Union harmonisation legislation (Annex I, Section A & B)
annex_I.json.relevance: Determines Annex I pathway for high-risk classification (Article 6(1)). Includes: Machinery, Medical Devices, IVD, Radio Equipment, Aviation, Vehicles, etc.

annex_II.json.title: List of criminal offences referred to in Article 5(1)(h)(iii) (Annex II)
annex_II.json.relevance: Enumerates the serious criminal offences (terrorism, trafficking, sexual exploitation, etc.) for which law enforcement may use real-time remote biometric identification in public spaces under the narrow exception in Article 5(1)(h)(iii).

annex_III.json.title: High-Risk AI systems — 8 domains (Article 6(2))
annex_III.json.relevance: PRIMARY reference for classifying standalone high-risk AI systems.

annex_IV.json.title: Technical documentation (Article 11)
annex_IV.json.relevance: Specifies what must be in technical documentation for high-risk AI providers.

annex_VI.json.title: Internal control conformity assessment procedure (Article 43(1)(a))
annex_VI.json.relevance: Self-assessment procedure for high-risk AI not requiring notified body.

annex_VII.json.title: Notified body conformity assessment (Article 43(1)(b))
annex_VII.json.relevance: Third-party assessment based on quality management system.

annex_VIII.json.title: EU database registration information (Article 49 & 71)
annex_VIII.json.relevance: Data fields required for EU database registration.

annex_IX.json.title: Real-world testing registration information (Annex IX — Article 60(4))
annex_IX.json.relevance: Information fields that providers must register in the EU database (Art 71) when conducting real-world testing outside AI regulatory sandboxes.

annex_X.json.title: List of Union law on large-scale biometric and border IT systems (Annex X)
annex_X.json.relevance: Enumerates the Union regulations on SIS, Eurodac, EES, ETIAS, VIS and related border/immigration IT systems. Relevant context for Annex III point 1 (biometric identification) and point 7 (migration, asylum and border control management) high-risk categories.

annex_XI.json.title: Technical documentation for GPAI models (Annex XI — Article 53(1)(a))
annex_XI.json.relevance: Minimum content requirements for the technical documentation that GPAI providers must draw up, keep up-to-date, and provide to the AI Office and national competent authorities on request.

annex_XII.json.title: Information to be provided to downstream providers (Annex XII — Article 53(1)(b))
annex_XII.json.relevance: Minimum elements GPAI providers must share with downstream AI system providers to enable them to understand the model's capabilities and limitations and comply with their own obligations.

annex_XIII.json.title: Criteria for designation of GPAI models with systemic risk (Annex XIII — Article 51 and Article 52(4))
annex_XIII.json.relevance: Criteria the Commission uses to assess whether a GPAI model has capabilities equivalent to the 10^25 FLOPs threshold, including parameters, training data size, computation, modalities, benchmarks, market reach, and registered end-users.

keyword_to_articles._description: Fast lookup: map domain keywords to relevant articles. Use as a first pass then follow the decision tree.

high_risk_provider_checklist.item: Risk management system documented and operational
Data governance framework in place; datasets validated
Instructions for use prepared for deployers (Article 13 requirements — no separate Annex in final Act)
Accuracy, robustness and cybersecurity tested and documented
Conformity assessment completed (self or notified body)
EU Declaration of Conformity signed (Annex V)
Post-market monitoring plan in place per Article 72 (Annex IX contains CE marking rules — it is not the post-market monitoring template)

high_risk_deployer_checklist.item: Use AI system strictly per provider instructions
Assign trained persons for human oversight
Monitor system in operation for anomalies
Retain automatically generated logs minimum 6 months
Inform provider of incidents or risks identified
Notify workers/representatives if workplace HR AI used
Conduct fundamental rights impact assessment (if required)

gpai_provider_checklist.item: Downstream provider information sheet per Annex XII
If systemic risk: adversarial testing / red-team completed
If systemic risk: serious incident reporting mechanism in place
If systemic risk: cybersecurity measures implemented
